title: My 6 Months with Claude Code: When AI Actually Lives Up to the Hype
author: Pierre-Alexandre Losson
text:
My 6 Months with Claude Code: When AI Actually Lives Up to the Hype
A brutally honest take on AI-powered development
After six months of using Claude Code daily in my work, I’ve learned that AI coding tools are exactly what people say they are — but only if you know what you’re doing.
The Great Divide: Juniors vs. Seniors
Let me be blunt: AI coding tools in junior hands can be dangerous. I’ve seen it firsthand. Junior developers ask “How do I do this?” and Claude happily spits out code that looks perfect but is actually a disaster waiting to happen. No guardrails, no understanding of why something works, just blind copy-paste.
It’s fine for personal tinkering, but in production? Hard no.
But flip that around — put these tools in experienced hands, and it’s game over. I’m talking 10x productivity gains. Not 10%, not 20% — we’re talking 900% increases. It’s radical.
The Learning Curve (It’s Worth It)
It took me a few months to really master this workflow, but once you understand both the power and limitations of LLMs, everything changes. You spend time describing your reality — translating years of experience into focused context. You explain what you want and, crucially, what you don’t want.
Once you nail that down, the output is incredible. You think of the next step, prompt it, and it’s done. The only limit becomes how fast you can come up with ideas.
But here’s the catch — you need to be good at execution and step-by-step thinking. It’s still software development, just at lightning speed.
And here’s the kicker: it’s not just about doing things faster. Claude Code lets you tackle projects you would NEVER have attempted manually. The scope of what becomes possible is mind-blowing.
Architecture: Your Make-or-Break Decision
Without proper architecture, you have two options:
- AI-only code that you’ll never be able to touch again
- Very small prototypes or throwaway projects
Or you get lucky with a framework that provides inherent structure (like CLI tools with Rich Click)
With architecture, everything changes. But you have to stay in the loop:
- Start with your PRD — clear objectives
- Design a TRD — architecture design and guidelines (not coding yet!)
- Create a CLAUDE.md — your AI’s context primer
Validation Is Everything
Always give AI the means to validate success. If proper code means tested and linted code, tell the AI how to run tests and lints — and make it do this after every single step.
The moment the LLM goes off track, you want to catch it immediately. Trust me on this.
One controversial take: don’t let the LLM write your tests. I know writing tests sucks, but unless you perfectly describe what you want tested, you’ll just get “make sure it runs” tests with no real value. Have it build the test foundation, but write the meaningful tests yourself.
The Sweet Spot and Gotchas
Context length matters. Too short and it’s useless. Too long and quality tanks. There’s a sweet spot you’ll learn to feel.
Always use plan mode. Iterate on the plan, especially in architecture-heavy projects. Review everything before letting it loose.
And here’s something weird — even when you explicitly tell Claude to do something specific, sometimes it just… doesn’t. You’ll learn to work around this quirk.
Real-World War Stories
The TypeScript to Python Migration: Had a full CLI written in TypeScript that needed to move to Python. Asked Claude to handle it. Did a wonderful job. Would never have even attempted this migration manually — the scope was just too daunting.
The Legacy Code Feature: Needed to add localized features to a 15-year-old C/C++ codebase. With the right tools (compile and test capabilities), Claude delivered a fully functional feature in 45 minutes. Would have taken an experienced developer days, maybe weeks — not due to lack of skill, but just the time needed to understand the ins and outs of that ancient codebase.
The Complete Rewrite: This was my best experience yet. Had a hand-coded Python project where architectural principles had finally emerged, and I wanted to redo it properly as the first version codebase had become a mess. The key was taking time to get my thoughts together, having crystal-clear architecture, and explaining how those principles translate to actual code. Then it became step-by-step capability building, with Claude validating, linting, and testing at each mini-step. This is literally software engineering on steroids.
The Bottom Line
Six months in, Claude Code has fundamentally changed not just how I work, but what I’m willing to tackle. It’s not magic — it’s a superpower that amplifies what you already know. If you’re experienced and methodical, it’s transformative. If you’re not… well, maybe stick to asking “Is this correct?” for now.
The future of development isn’t AI replacing developers. It’s experienced developers moving at superhuman speed and taking on impossible projects.
Share your experience with Claude Code (or other LLM’s & AI coding tools), I’d love to get real world feedback from others.
